rules.summary: Source paths expose named capabilities instead of generic technical layers.
rules.rationale: Capability ownership keeps behavior and its policy discoverable together.

rules.summary: Generic bucket filenames are forbidden.
rules.rationale: A filename should identify the domain job a reader will find inside.

rules.summary: Authored directories and non-component files use camelCase and never repeat role suffixes.
rules.rationale: One predictable authored name avoids encoded layer tax such as order.repository.ts.

rules.summary: Pass-through service and wrapper layers are forbidden.
rules.rationale: A local abstraction must own policy or a real external boundary.

rules.summary: Internal import cycles are forbidden.
rules.rationale: Acyclic capability dependencies keep ownership and initialization deterministic.
rules.goodExample: cli imports install; install does not import cli.
rules.badExample: catalog imports config and config imports catalog.

rules.summary: Bindings use const unless the binding itself is reassigned.
rules.rationale: Const makes rebinding intent explicit while still allowing locally owned collection mutation.
rules.badExample: let results = [] when results is never reassigned.

rules.summary: Named functions are arrow constants declared before use.
rules.rationale: Lexical declarations make execution order readable without hoisting.

rules.summary: Only an anonymous generator directly passed to Effect.gen is allowed.
rules.rationale: The exception keeps dependent Effect workflows readable without reopening function declarations.

rules.summary: Use cohesive inputs, at most two natural positional values, and no boolean behavior flags.
rules.rationale: Named requests make three-way coordination explicit without ceremonial single-value wrappers.
rules.badExample: const install = (root, feature, force, dryRun) => program;

rules.summary: Each function owns one visible domain job.
rules.rationale: Cohesive bodies are easier to verify than mixed policy, I/O, and presentation.

rules.summary: One-expression pass-through helpers need a concept, policy, boundary, or second caller.
rules.rationale: Names that add no information force readers to jump without reducing complexity.
rules.badExample: const getName = (item) => item.name; used once immediately below

rules.summary: Adjacent functions have one blank line between them.
rules.rationale: A single visual boundary separates declarations without vertical noise.

rules.summary: Control flow nests no more than two levels.
rules.rationale: Guard clauses preserve a straight-line reading path.
rules.goodExample: Return early before the main path.
rules.badExample: Three nested if or loop blocks.

rules.summary: Schema.TaggedError is the only authored class form.
rules.rationale: Data, functions, and official services are the default; expected errors keep executable schemas.

rules.applicability: Defect handling and public error boundaries
rules.summary: Preserve defect causes internally and translate or redact once at the public boundary.
rules.rationale: Operators need the original diagnostic chain while callers receive stable, non-sensitive errors.
rules.goodExample: A tagged internal failure retains cause; the CLI boundary renders one safe translated message.
rules.badExample: Catch, discard cause, and leak a provider response or stack to the caller.

rules.summary: Only a non-obvious loop receives an immediate intent or invariant comment.
rules.rationale: Comments explain ordering, batching, mutation, performance, or early exit rather than narrating syntax.
rules.goodExample:
// Preserve receipt order during rollback.
for (const artifact of reversed) { ... }
rules.badExample:
// Loop through items.
for (const item of items) { print(item); }

rules.summary: A non-obvious indexed access has an immediate proof comment and no assertion.
rules.rationale: The proof records why the index is safe while TypeScript remains honest.
rules.goodExample:
// The bounds guard proves index is present.
return items[index];

rules.applicability: Ordered workflows with three or more dependent phases
rules.summary: A real ordered pipeline has one contract and numbered phase comments.
rules.rationale: The contract makes ordering and failure ownership reviewable at the orchestration point.
rules.goodExample: Contract comment plus // 1., // 2., and // 3. before dependent phases.
rules.badExample: A long Effect.gen workflow with unexplained ordering.

rules.summary: TSDoc exists only when it adds caller-relevant contract information.
rules.rationale: Documentation should explain behavior, ownership, errors, ordering, security, or performance instead of restating syntax.
rules.goodExample: /** Deletes only paths proven owned by the receipt. */
rules.badExample: /** Gets the name. @param item The item. @returns The name. */

rules.summary: Effect Schema owns validation, derived types, descriptions, messages, and generated contracts.
rules.rationale: One executable source prevents drift between runtime validation and TypeScript claims.
rules.badExample: type Feature = { id: string }; const featureSchema = validator(...);

rules.summary: Interfaces are limited to declaration augmentation and earned feature-owned external capability ports.
rules.rationale: Runtime objects come from schemas; substitutable external capabilities may need structural ports.
rules.goodExample: A feature adapter owns GitHubPort with multiple substitutable operations.

rules.summary: Enums are forbidden.
rules.rationale: Schema literals provide runtime validation and a derived union from one source.

rules.summary: Authored conditional and infer machinery is forbidden.
rules.rationale: Direct schema-derived types are clearer than recovering types from descriptors.
rules.badExample: type Item<T> = T extends Array<infer U> ? U : T

rules.summary: Type assertions and non-null assertions are forbidden.
rules.rationale: Decode or narrow unknown values so runtime evidence supports the type.

rules.summary: Type, lint, format, and coverage suppression directives are forbidden.
rules.rationale: The underlying boundary must remain visible and fixed.
rules.goodExample: Narrow the value and keep the rule active.

rules.summary: Use undefined for ordinary absence, protocol null only at adapters, and Option for business absence.
rules.rationale: One value should not carry three competing absence representations.
rules.goodExample: Option<User> in policy; null translated inside an HTTP adapter.
rules.badExample: User | null | undefined in application code.

rules.summary: Use named exports unless a framework requires a default export.
rules.rationale: Named imports remain searchable and refactorable across internal modules.

rules.summary: Internal barrels and every export star are forbidden.
rules.rationale: Direct imports expose real ownership and prevent hidden dependency fan-out.

rules.summary: Identifiers use domain jobs instead of vague manager, helper, utils, data, or info roles.
rules.rationale: A specific name tells readers what policy or value the owner provides.

rules.summary: A split needs an independent change reason, second caller, or genuine domain concept.
rules.rationale: One-export files and speculative layers add navigation without isolating change.
rules.goodExample: artifactPlan.ts owns its schema and planning operations.

rules.summary: Inputs are never mutated.
rules.rationale: Borrowed values retain stable ownership across a call boundary.

rules.summary: Local push, Map.set, and Set.add are allowed when the function owns the collection.
rules.rationale: Contained mutation can make a multi-step algorithm clearer without leaking state.
rules.badExample: Mutating an array received from a caller.

rules.summary: Reduce is not used to build arrays or objects.
rules.rationale: Map, filter, flatMap, Object.fromEntries, or a local loop states construction more directly.

rules.summary: Use collection methods for direct transforms and for-of for branching or local multi-step state.
rules.rationale: The construct should expose the algorithm rather than satisfy a blanket preference.
rules.goodExample: items.map(render) or a documented multi-step for-of.
rules.badExample: A stateful map callback or ceremonial loop.

rules.summary: One dependent handoff may use one flatMap; two or more use Effect.gen.
rules.rationale: The composition form should expose dependency depth without a flatMap pyramid.
rules.goodExample: load.pipe(Effect.flatMap(decode)) or Effect.gen for load, decode, and persist.

rules.summary: Application code does not use Promise.all for effectful work.
rules.rationale: Effect operators preserve typed failures and explicit concurrency policy.

rules.summary: The main application has one Node runtime and context-provision edge in src/cli/main.ts.
rules.rationale: One deployable runtime edge preserves composition and lifecycle ownership.
rules.badExample: Effect.runPromise, NodeRuntime.runMain, or NodeContext.layer inside install.ts

rules.summary: Use official services directly unless a repository owner adds real policy.
rules.rationale: Renaming an official service behind a local tag adds indirection without control.
rules.badExample: yield* FileManager where every method delegates unchanged

rules.summary: Effectful collections are sequential unless bounded concurrency is justified.
rules.rationale: Filesystem and provider mutations need predictable ordering and load.

rules.summary: Hook graphs import only node modules, shared runtime transport, and their feature runtime.
rules.rationale: Installed hooks execute in foreign processes without package dependencies.
rules.goodExample: A contextGuard hook imports node:fs and ../runtime/decide.js.
rules.badExample: A hook imports effect or src/install.

rules.summary: Application modules do not import installed hook runtime code.
rules.rationale: Shared transport parsing and application orchestration remain separate owners.
rules.goodExample: Application and hook both consume a dependency-free wire format separately.

rules.summary: Application presentation routes through TerminalUI instead of console calls.
rules.rationale: One boundary owns TTY behavior, rendering, and structured non-interactive results.

rules.summary: Root scripts decode arguments, call one substantive owner, render, and set process status.
rules.rationale: Substantive tooling stays importable, testable, and co-located under src.
rules.badExample: A root script owns AST traversal, filesystem planning, and domain types.

rules.summary: Application source never imports repository scripts.
rules.rationale: Tool entrypoints depend on substantive owners, never the reverse.

rules.summary: Provider SDKs are confined to earned feature-owned adapter modules.
rules.rationale: A narrow adapter keeps provider shapes and failures out of domain operations.

rules.applicability: Maintained TypeScript, scripts, and machine contract JSON
rules.summary: Biome owns formatting, import organization, and its recommended lint rules.
rules.rationale: One proven formatter and linter removes subjective mechanical review.
rules.goodExample: Run biome check --write on an explicitly reviewed file list.

exceptions.reason: Preserve the owner's concurrent trailer assembly rewrite byte-for-byte.
exceptions.exitCondition: Remove after the owner commits and independently reviews the trailer rewrite.

exceptions.reason: Preserve the owner's concurrent trailer assembly rewrite byte-for-byte.
exceptions.exitCondition: Remove after the owner commits and independently reviews the trailer rewrite.

exceptions.reason: Preserve the owner's concurrent trailer assembly rewrite byte-for-byte.
exceptions.exitCondition: Remove after the owner commits and independently reviews the trailer rewrite.
